name: PostgreSQL 16
what: Open-source relational database and the canonical SQL backbone for many self-hosted web applications.
solves: System of record for transactional data, identity data, billing metadata, scheduling records, CRM sync state, and analytics-friendly event tables.
users: Engineering teams, product teams, and operators who need ACID semantics, mature tooling, and strong ecosystem support.
fit: Excellent fit. It already aligns with Next.js + Drizzle + Auth.js, and many candidate Hylono apps either require PostgreSQL directly or can use it as their preferred production datastore.
runtime: Single binary database server with WAL, background maintenance, replication options, and a large extension ecosystem.
components: Server process, storage volume, WAL archive/backups, optional connection pooler, metrics exporter, and optional replicas.
deploy: Managed service for site data or dedicated self-hosted instances for app groups that need local control, extension freedom, or network isolation.
deps: Fast persistent storage, reliable backups, monitoring, connection pooling for higher concurrency, and disciplined schema migration workflows.
datastores: PostgreSQL is the datastore.
auth: Native database roles, password or certificate auth, TLS, network controls, and application-scoped credentials.
apis: SQL over standard drivers, admin tooling, logical replication, backups/restores, and exporter endpoints for monitoring.
security: Tier-0 data service. Encrypt in transit, restrict network exposure, separate app credentials, test restores, and treat backup handling as a compliance control.
setup_path: Use official PostgreSQL 16 packages or containers; enable TLS, backups, autovacuum visibility, connection pooling, and metrics before broad app consolidation.
env: POSTGRES_DB, POSTGRES_USER, POSTGRES_PASSWORD, storage sizing, WAL retention, backup target credentials, and pooler settings.
persistence: Base backups plus WAL archiving; test restore regularly; keep schema migration discipline per app.
operations: Quarterly minor patching, vacuum/bloat review, replication monitoring, slow query visibility, and capacity checks for connection spikes.
failure_points: Connection exhaustion, slow disks, missing WAL retention, poor backup testing, and shared-cluster blast radius from noisy neighbors.
integration: Backbone for the Hylono Next.js app and a strong fit for ZITADEL, Kong, Cal.com, Medusa, Lago, Metabase, Appsmith, and other app databases.
verify: Decide whether Hylono will centralize multiple app schemas on shared PostgreSQL clusters or isolate high-risk apps into separate instances.
risk: Low technical risk but high blast radius. Treat as a core platform dependency and isolate backups, monitoring, and access control accordingly.
gdpr: Will almost certainly hold customer and business data. EU residency, backup geography, encryption, and retention policies matter.
overlap: None. This is a canonical foundational service.
codex_read_first: Read the release notes, current operational runbooks, and the per-app dependency map before consolidating more workloads onto PostgreSQL.
codex_best_path: Keep the public site on Neon/Postgres if that is already stable; add separate PostgreSQL instances or databases for self-hosted apps that do not belong in the main site database.
codex_never_assume: Never assume one shared PostgreSQL cluster is acceptable for every app. Verify extension needs, upgrade windows, backup RPO/RTO, and tenant isolation.

name: Redis 7
license: RSALv2 / SSPLv1 for 7.4.x–7.8.x; BSDv3 for older 7.2.x and earlier; Redis 8 adds AGPLv3 option
what: In-memory data structure server used for cache, queues, ephemeral state, rate limiting, and some workflow backplanes.
solves: Low-latency caching, job queues, session support, pub/sub or streams, rate limits, and burst absorption between systems.
users: Engineering and platform teams that need fast volatile storage or queue primitives for web apps and automation systems.
fit: Strong technical fit because many candidate apps use Redis for queues or cache, but the licensing transition requires deliberate human review before expanding use.
runtime: Single process server with persistence options, replication/sentinel/cluster modes, and multiple data structures including lists, streams, and sorted sets.
components: Redis server, persistent volume if AOF/RDB enabled, optional sentinel/cluster peers, application clients, and observability hooks.
deploy: Single-node with persistence for smaller app clusters; HA or managed equivalent only when justified by queue criticality and recovery targets.
deps: RAM headroom, persistent storage if durability matters, monitoring, auth/TLS, and app-level retry logic.
datastores: Redis is the datastore; often paired with PostgreSQL or MongoDB.
auth: ACLs, passwords, TLS, network segmentation, and client-specific credentials.
apis: RESP protocol, libraries, pub/sub, streams, queues, and exporter metrics.
security: Network exposure is dangerous. Use TLS or private networking, ACLs, secret rotation, and memory/eviction monitoring.
setup_path: Use official Redis 7 packages or containers only after licensing is approved internally; enable persistence only where required and document eviction policy explicitly.
env: REDIS_PASSWORD, appendonly/save settings, maxmemory, eviction policy, TLS certs, and network binding.
persistence: Choose AOF/RDB deliberately. Do not pretend volatile cache and durable queue workloads have the same recovery requirements.
operations: Memory pressure review, eviction monitoring, persistence checks, replica health, and version/license review before upgrades.
failure_points: OOM kills, silent key eviction, unauthenticated network exposure, and using Redis as a durable queue without operational guardrails.
integration: Needed by Novu, n8n queue mode, and many web apps for caches or background work. It fits the stack, but licensing must be surfaced early to legal and procurement stakeholders.
verify: Confirm whether Hylono is comfortable with Redis 7 licensing, whether Valkey is a preferable future path, and which workloads genuinely require Redis rather than database-backed queues.
risk: Moderate platform risk because it can become a hidden dependency and the licensing change affects procurement and redistribution decisions.
gdpr: Usually stores transient operational data, but payloads may still contain personal data if queue design is sloppy. Minimize and expire sensitive values.
recommendation: KEEP FOR EXISTING USE; REQUIRE LICENSE REVIEW BEFORE EXPANSION
overlap: Possible future substitution candidate with Valkey for some workloads.
codex_read_first: Read the licensing page and the actual app dependencies before treating Redis as a default choice.
codex_best_path: Retain Redis for apps that explicitly need it, but avoid adding new business-critical designs that depend on Redis durability without a clear recovery plan.
codex_never_assume: Never assume all Redis 7 builds are permissively licensed. Never assume cache settings are safe for queue workloads.

name: MongoDB 7
version_status: CONFIRMED line / point release partly UNVERIFIED
what: Document database for flexible JSON-like data models, high-ingest workloads, and applications that expect MongoDB specifically.
solves: Schema-flexible application data, event capture, content documents, and vendor apps that are built around MongoDB.
users: Engineering teams and packaged applications that prefer document-oriented models or require MongoDB for supportability.
fit: Selective fit. Hylono’s primary site stack is more naturally PostgreSQL-centric, but MongoDB remains relevant because Novu and some app ecosystems expect it.
runtime: Single database server or replica set/sharded cluster with BSON document storage, indexes, background maintenance, and replication.
components: mongod, optional mongos/shards, persistent storage, backups, metrics, and client libraries.
deploy: Replica set for production durability; single node only for labs or low-risk internal services.
deps: Fast storage, memory headroom, backups, monitoring, and version-compatible drivers.
datastores: MongoDB is the datastore.
auth: SCRAM/X.509, RBAC, TLS, network controls, and app-scoped users.
apis: Mongo wire protocol, drivers, backup/restore tooling, metrics, and change streams in supported setups.
security: Treat as a production database: TLS, private networking, least-privilege users, and backup encryption.
setup_path: Use official 7.0 production docs. Prefer replica sets for any meaningful workload. Plan backups, monitoring, and index review from day one.
env: MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME, MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD, replica-set config, storage sizing, backup target credentials.
persistence: Snapshot plus oplog-aware backup strategy; test restores; watch index growth.
operations: Patch within the 7.0 line, review slow queries/indexes, monitor replication lag, and capacity-plan storage.
failure_points: Undersized storage, replica misconfiguration, index sprawl, and using MongoDB where relational guarantees were actually needed.
integration: Most relevant as a dependency for Novu or any app that is explicitly Mongo-first. It should not become the default datastore for the Hylono web platform.
verify: Confirm which apps truly require MongoDB and whether those should run in isolated clusters instead of sharing with unrelated workloads.
risk: Moderate. Operationally manageable, but it introduces another database family into the platform.
gdpr: Any personal data stored here requires the same EU residency, encryption, and retention discipline as PostgreSQL.
overlap: Partial overlap with PostgreSQL for general application storage, but not a replacement for Mongo-dependent apps.
codex_read_first: Read the app-specific dependency notes before assuming MongoDB belongs in a new design.
codex_best_path: Use MongoDB only for apps that explicitly depend on it; avoid inventing new Hylono domain models on MongoDB unless there is a concrete reason.
codex_never_assume: Never assume MongoDB should be the default because it is already running. Verify supportability and data-model trade-offs first.

name: MinIO
version: Community repo archived; AIStor docs active
version_status: CONFIRMED strategic change / exact CE status requires human review
license: Historically AGPLv3 for community code; current AIStor materials emphasize commercial licensing
license_status: CONFIRMED strategic licensing shift / exact packaging requires human review
what: S3-compatible object storage previously common in self-hosted stacks for files, backups, and media.
solves: Internal object storage for uploads, documents, backups, artifacts, and application binary payloads.
users: Infrastructure teams that need S3-compatible storage under their own control.
fit: Technically useful, but strategically risky for new standardization because the public community repo appears archived and the official site/docs now center on commercial AIStor.
runtime: Distributed or single-node object storage service with S3 API compatibility, erasure coding, and policy controls.
components: Storage nodes, TLS, access policies, buckets, backup/replication design, and monitoring.
deploy: Historically container- or Kubernetes-friendly; current official guidance is centered on AIStor and its operator/helm-based patterns.
deps: Durable disks, network throughput, TLS, backup/replication design, S3-compatible client validation.
datastores: Object data on disk; metadata managed by the service.
auth: Access keys, policies, TLS, and admin controls.
apis: S3-compatible API, console/admin endpoints, replication and lifecycle features.
security: Object storage becomes a central data plane. Misconfigured buckets, replication, or lifecycle rules create serious exposure.
setup_path: Do not assume legacy MinIO community guidance still matches the 2026 reality. Revalidate whether Hylono wants MinIO specifically, an AIStor commercial commitment, or another object-storage path.
env: Root credentials, domain/bucket config, storage layout, TLS certs, and policy settings.
persistence: Object storage durability depends on storage design, replication, and tested backup/restore procedures.
operations: Capacity monitoring, lifecycle rule validation, bucket policy review, and strategic vendor/license review.
failure_points: Assuming community MinIO remains a stable default, underestimating bucket-policy risk, and building new dependencies before clarifying the product/licensing direction.
integration: Would be useful for Documenso/DocuSeal documents, Medusa assets, Listmonk media, backups, and possibly Next.js uploads—but only after the product/licensing direction is settled.
verify: Verify whether Hylono already uses MinIO, whether that deployment is supportable, and whether a different object storage platform is preferable for new rollout work.
risk: High strategic risk for new adoption because of the apparent community/archive/commercial shift.
gdpr: Object storage may contain signed documents, uploads, backups, and audit artifacts. EU hosting, encryption, retention, and access logging matter.
recommendation: DO NOT EXPAND UNTIL PRODUCT/LICENSE DIRECTION IS DECIDED
overlap: Can be replaced by managed S3-compatible storage or alternative self-hosted object stores.
codex_read_first: Read the official site/docs state and repository status before coding against MinIO assumptions.
codex_best_path: If object storage is needed immediately, verify the currently running environment first; otherwise keep the abstraction S3-compatible so the backend can be swapped.
codex_never_assume: Never assume legacy MinIO CE deployment advice is still the preferred path for a new 2026 deployment.

sources.why: Used to inspect public repo status and recent release history.

name: Uptime Kuma
what: Self-hosted uptime monitoring and status page tool for HTTP, TCP, ping, and application health endpoints.
solves: Basic uptime checks, certificate expiry alerts, and operator-facing status visibility without standing up a heavier observability stack first.
users: Ops and engineering teams that need lightweight service monitoring and status pages.
fit: Good fit as a low-friction operational layer for early rollout, especially while the stack is still expanding and formal observability is being layered in.
runtime: Node.js application with local/attached storage and outbound notification integrations.
components: Web UI, monitor definitions, notification channels, persistent storage, and optional public status page.
deploy: Docker is the normal path; single-node deployment is common.
deps: Persistent volume, outbound notification channel, reverse proxy, and backup of monitor configuration.
datastores: Internal application storage (SQLite or configured database depending on deployment mode).
auth: Local users; protect with reverse proxy/TLS and strong credentials.
apis: HTTP checks, notifications, status page URLs, and import/export features.
security: Keep the admin UI private, patch regularly, and avoid exposing internal-only endpoints publicly through status pages.
setup_path: Deploy via official container, reverse-proxy behind TLS, configure notifications, and import monitors for all tier-0 services.
env: Port, base URL, reverse-proxy headers, persistence path, and notification credentials.
persistence: Back up monitor config and internal database/volume.
operations: Add monitors as new apps go live, test notifications, and patch on release cadence.
failure_points: Treating it as a full observability platform, forgetting backups, or exposing sensitive internal target details on public status pages.
integration: Useful immediately for public site, APIs, Kong, auth endpoints, key app front doors, and certificate checks.
verify: Decide whether Hylono wants a public status page or strictly internal monitoring.
risk: Low. Lightweight and easy to replace if observability matures.
gdpr: Minimal direct data risk unless notifications or public pages expose user data.
overlap: Overlaps slightly with Prometheus/Grafana alerting but provides faster operator value for basic uptime checks.
codex_read_first: Read the deployment docs and decide which endpoints should be monitored privately versus exposed on a status page.
codex_best_path: Use it as the first visibility layer while Prometheus/Grafana mature underneath.
codex_never_assume: Never assume Uptime Kuma replaces metrics, traces, logs, or security monitoring.

name: Twenty CRM
license: Mostly GPL-3.0 (verify repo licensing scope before bundling code)
what: Open-source CRM focused on people, companies, opportunities, pipelines, tasks, and CRM customization.
solves: Lead and account management, opportunity tracking, relationship context, activity history, and CRM workflow customization.
users: Revenue teams, founders, operators, and engineering-led teams that want a self-hostable CRM with modern APIs and UI.
fit: High fit. Hylono already cares about Twenty integration, and it aligns well with lead capture, partnership tracking, booking-to-sales follow-up, and premium customer lifecycle management.
runtime: Web application with API layer, background processes, database, storage, and extension/webhook surfaces.
components: Frontend, API, PostgreSQL-backed data layer, worker/background jobs, file storage, and webhook/API interfaces.
deploy: Official Docker Compose is the pragmatic path for self-hosting; production hardening needs persistent storage, backups, reverse proxy, and upgrade discipline.
deps: PostgreSQL, storage, mail/outbound integrations, and likely background workers depending on deployment mode.
datastores: PostgreSQL primary datastore; storage for files and imports/exports.
auth: Built-in auth with workspace/users; evaluate SSO path against ZITADEL if needed.
apis: REST and GraphQL APIs, webhooks, imports/exports, and extension/developer surfaces.
security: CRM data will include customer and partner context. Access control, auditability, backup encryption, and GDPR-ready retention matter.
setup_path: Use official self-host Docker Compose path first; validate API/webhook behavior in a non-production workspace before wiring site automations.
env: DB URL, app URLs, auth secrets, mailer credentials, storage settings, webhook/API keys, and worker-related config.
persistence: Back up the PostgreSQL database and any file storage; test export/restore of CRM data.
operations: Track upstream schema changes, API stability, webhook payload changes, and license scope before deep customization.
failure_points: Over-customizing before core schemas stabilize, mixing source-of-truth ownership across systems, and ignoring GDPR retention/export needs.
integration: Strong touchpoints with Next.js contact/booking/rental flows, n8n for orchestration, Novu for notifications, Stripe/rental operations, and PostHog or Metabase for lifecycle analytics.
verify: Define system-of-record boundaries: what lives in Twenty versus the Hylono core app database, and whether sales workflow customization should be done in CRM or automation layer.
risk: Moderate. Valuable and implementable, but CRM data-model ownership can become messy if sync rules are weak.
gdpr: Contains identifiable customer/prospect data. EU hosting, consent basis, retention, export/delete workflows, and access controls are mandatory.
overlap: Partial overlap with custom internal admin or lightweight lead tables in the site; Twenty should likely be the primary CRM rather than rebuilding CRM features in Next.js.
codex_read_first: Read the official developer docs, API/webhook references, and current Hylono API routes before mapping lead and booking events.
codex_best_path: Use Twenty as CRM of record for leads/accounts/opportunities; keep the Next.js site focused on capture, identity, commerce, and operational workflows. Synchronize through n8n or explicit server actions/webhooks.
codex_never_assume: Never assume site tables and CRM objects have identical schema or lifecycle ownership. Never assume webhook payloads or API scopes without testing against the running version.

sources.why: Supports API, webhook, and self-host guidance.

name: Novu
version: Platform v3.13.0 observed; JS SDK v3.14.0 also current
license: MIT core with commercial/open-core enterprise layers
what: Notification infrastructure platform for email, SMS, push, in-app, and chat notifications with workflows, providers, and templates.
solves: Centralized transactional notification orchestration, provider abstraction, templates, preference handling, and event-driven workflow logic.
users: Product teams and platform teams that want one notification layer rather than bespoke channel logic in every application.
fit: Very high fit. Hylono already uses Novu, and it aligns with booking events, checkout updates, rental lifecycle messaging, service reminders, support notifications, and internal ops alerts.
runtime: API/service layer with worker processes, Redis, MongoDB, storage, provider connectors, and workflow/template management.
components: API, dashboard, workers, Redis, MongoDB, outbound channel providers, templates, and webhooks.
deploy: Self-host via official guides on VMs/containers; production deployments need Redis, MongoDB, storage, secrets, and provider configuration.
deps: MongoDB, Redis, storage, outbound providers, TLS/reverse proxy, and worker scaling.
datastores: MongoDB plus Redis; object/file storage depending on feature use.
auth: API keys, dashboard auth, workspace/user controls, and provider credentials.
apis: REST APIs, SDKs, webhooks, event triggers, templates, and provider integrations.
security: Notification payloads can contain PII and order/service context. Keep provider creds in a secrets system, scope API keys, and minimize sensitive data in templates.
setup_path: Start from official self-host overview, validate required MongoDB/Redis/storage dependencies, and test outbound providers end-to-end before production use.
env: Mongo URL, Redis URL, storage config, API keys, JWT/app secrets, provider credentials, and base URLs.
persistence: Back up MongoDB, preserve template/workflow definitions, and version control IaC for deployment settings.
operations: Watch queue backlogs, provider failures, webhook retries, payload drift, and version compatibility across API/dashboard/workers.
failure_points: Assuming provider abstraction removes deliverability work, under-sizing worker/queue capacity, and storing too much sensitive context in notification payloads.
integration: Direct fit with Next.js API routes, Stripe events, rental flows, Cal.com bookings, CRM updates, n8n workflows, and operational alerting.
verify: Define which notifications stay in Novu versus Resend-only direct sends, and whether customer preferences live in Novu, the site database, or both.
risk: Moderate. High leverage, but notification systems fail noisily if payload ownership, templates, or provider ops are weak.
gdpr: Contains user contact details and event context. Respect consent/preferences, minimize sensitive content, and prefer EU-hosted supporting infrastructure where possible.
overlap: Overlaps with direct Resend sends, basic n8n notifications, Listmonk campaigns, and Mautic journeys, but Novu should remain the transactional notification backbone.
codex_read_first: Read the self-host overview, API/webhook references, and current Hylono messaging flows before adding new templates or events.
codex_best_path: Use Novu as the event-driven transactional layer and keep channel provider credentials outside application code. Feed it from site events and orchestration workflows.
codex_never_assume: Never assume template variables, provider support, or webhook/event semantics without testing on the current platform version.

name: Medusa
what: Open-source commerce platform for carts, checkout, product catalog, orders, inventory, and admin workflows.
solves: Headless commerce backend when Hylono needs richer ecommerce than a marketing-focused Next.js site with basic Stripe checkout.
users: Commerce teams and engineering teams that want a self-hostable backend with custom storefronts and workflows.
fit: Potentially strong, but conditional. Hylono already has Stripe and server-first Next.js. Medusa is only worth the complexity if product/catalog/order workflows outgrow the current custom site architecture.
runtime: Node.js commerce engine with admin, APIs, database, workers/modules, and optional separate storefront.
components: Backend server, admin dashboard, PostgreSQL, Redis (depending on modules/workers), file storage, and a separate Next.js storefront or custom frontend.
deploy: Self-host backend separately from the public site; use the official Next.js starter as reference, not a reason to refactor the whole public marketing app into a commerce monolith.
deps: PostgreSQL, optional Redis, storage provider, payment connectors (Stripe), search/indexing if scale grows, and admin/auth configuration.
datastores: PostgreSQL primary; optional Redis and external storage/search integrations.
auth: Admin/customer auth model plus API keys and provider integrations.
apis: REST/GraphQL-style commerce APIs, webhooks/events, admin APIs, storefront SDKs, payment/shipping integrations.
security: Commerce systems hold customer/order/payment-adjacent data. Segment responsibilities, harden admin access, and verify tax/price/rental edge cases before rollout.
setup_path: Use official backend install/self-host path plus the official Next.js starter only as a reference implementation. Keep Medusa isolated behind APIs until it proves necessary.
env: DATABASE_URL, REDIS_URL if used, STRIPE keys, storage credentials, admin/auth secrets, base URLs, and worker config.
persistence: Back up PostgreSQL and file storage; preserve migration discipline and admin config.
operations: Track module compatibility, storefront/backend version alignment, payment webhook correctness, and admin access controls.
failure_points: Prematurely replacing a working server-first site architecture, duplicating catalog/order logic, and underestimating migration work from custom Stripe flows.
integration: Strong candidate if Hylono wants deeper catalog, bundles, inventory, and order workflows. Integrates with Stripe, PostHog, Novu, n8n, search, and internal analytics.
verify: Confirm whether current checkout/rental flows truly require a full commerce backend. Also verify whether rentals fit Medusa cleanly or need custom domain logic outside classic ecommerce.
risk: Moderate to high because commerce replatforming can sprawl.
gdpr: Customer/order data and event logs require standard EU data controls; avoid unnecessary duplication between Medusa and the core site DB.
overlap: Overlaps with existing Stripe-powered custom flows; only adopt if Hylono needs a stronger commerce core.
codex_read_first: Read the current site checkout/rental architecture and Medusa’s official Next.js starter before proposing a replatform.
codex_best_path: Prototype Medusa as a separate commerce service on a subdomain or internal environment. Prove catalog/order benefits before changing public-site architecture.
codex_never_assume: Never assume Medusa should own the whole frontend. Never assume standard ecommerce models cover Hylono rental/protocol workflows without extension work.

name: Lago
what: Open-source billing, metering, subscriptions, invoicing, and usage-based pricing platform.
solves: Usage metering, recurring billing logic, invoices, credits, pricing plans, and finance-facing billing operations that are cumbersome to build from scratch.
users: SaaS/product operators and finance/ops teams that need sophisticated billing beyond direct Stripe checkout.
fit: Potentially useful if Hylono introduces memberships, device access plans, protocol subscriptions, or usage-based/rental billing complexity. For straightforward Stripe commerce it may be heavier than needed.
runtime: Web/API application with billing engine, workers, database, and connectors.
components: API/UI, billing engine, background jobs, PostgreSQL, message/queue layer depending on deployment, and external payment/invoicing integrations.
deploy: Official Docker Compose and Helm paths exist. Production requires persistent database, background processing, mail/notifications, and finance-grade change control.
deps: PostgreSQL, background workers/queues, email/notifications, webhooks, and payment provider integration.
datastores: PostgreSQL primary datastore.
auth: Built-in auth plus API keys and finance/admin roles.
apis: REST APIs, webhooks, billing events, metering ingestion, invoice/subscription management.
security: Billing systems directly affect revenue and customer trust. Webhook integrity, tax/invoice correctness, and finance-process controls matter.
setup_path: Start with official self-host docs and Docker Compose only after a billing domain model is agreed. Do not deploy speculatively.
env: DB URL, app secrets, payment provider credentials, webhooks, mailer config, storage if needed, and background job settings.
persistence: Back up the billing DB and preserve invoice artifacts. Test webhook replay and invoice regeneration assumptions carefully.
operations: Version upgrades require billing regression checks, webhook validation, and finance-side acceptance criteria.
failure_points: Overlapping responsibilities with Stripe, introducing invoice truth conflicts, and deploying metering before the business pricing model is stable.
integration: Could connect to Stripe, Next.js checkout/rental APIs, n8n automation, Twenty CRM account data, Novu notifications, and Metabase finance reporting.
verify: Define whether Hylono actually needs usage/subscription billing beyond current Stripe capabilities. Decide whether Lago or Invoice Ninja should own invoice generation if both are present.
risk: High domain-model risk if introduced too early.
gdpr: Contains customer billing data; treat as financial system of record if adopted.
overlap: Overlaps partly with Stripe Billing and Invoice Ninja/Akaunting depending on scope.
codex_read_first: Read current Hylono pricing, rental, and checkout flows before proposing Lago implementation.
codex_best_path: Use Lago only when subscription/usage billing is a confirmed requirement; isolate it as a dedicated billing service, not a casual add-on.
codex_never_assume: Never assume finance will accept a new billing source of truth without reconciliation, tax, and invoice workflow validation.

name: Snipe-IT
what: Open-source IT asset management tool for hardware, accessories, consumables, licenses, and user/device assignment.
solves: Internal asset inventory, check-in/check-out, depreciation, and auditability for organizational equipment.
users: IT and operations teams managing internal equipment fleets.
fit: Limited for customer-facing rentals. Useful if Hylono mainly wants internal device/office asset control, but Leihs is a better fit for rental/lending workflows.
runtime: Laravel/PHP web app with database, mail, queues/scheduled jobs, and file uploads.
components: Web UI/API, relational database, mailer, cron/jobs, storage, and auth integrations like LDAP/SSO options.
deploy: Commonly self-hosted via Docker or standard PHP stack behind a reverse proxy.
deps: Database, PHP runtime, mailer, storage, cron/scheduler, and optional LDAP/AD integrations.
datastores: Relational database (commonly MySQL/MariaDB).
auth: Local accounts plus optional LDAP/AD; review SSO options if needed.
apis: REST API, import/export, reports, and asset/audit workflows.
security: Contains inventory records, user assignments, and audit data. Standard web-app hardening and backup controls are required.
setup_path: Follow official install docs, pick a supported DB/runtime combination, and verify whether internal IT asset scope justifies separate deployment from rental tooling.
env: APP_URL, DB settings, mailer config, app key, cron, storage path, and LDAP settings if used.
persistence: Back up database and uploaded asset files.
operations: Laravel/PHP upgrades, cron reliability, import quality, and access-role review.
failure_points: Trying to bend it into a customer rental system, or letting asset ownership drift between Snipe-IT and other systems.
integration: Best used only for internal Hylono equipment if needed. Limited direct value to the public site beyond admin links or reporting extracts.
verify: Clarify whether Hylono needs internal IT asset management separately from customer rental/loan workflows.
risk: Low to moderate if scoped internally; poor fit if stretched into rental operations.
gdpr: Mostly operational asset data, but assigned-user records still count as personal data.
overlap: Leihs covers lending/rental better; Snipe-IT is for internal asset control.
codex_read_first: Read Leihs and Snipe-IT packages together before choosing an asset/rental platform.
codex_best_path: Use Snipe-IT only if internal IT asset management is a distinct need separate from customer equipment lending.
codex_never_assume: Never assume Snipe-IT is the right backbone for customer-facing rentals.

name: Leihs
researched_as: Leihs equipment lending and inventory system
license: AGPL-3.0 (verify current repo before bundling code)
what: Open-source equipment booking, inventory, procurement, and lending system designed around loan workflows.
solves: Reservation, lending, returns, inventory visibility, and operational tracking for equipment that moves between users.
users: Organizations managing loaner equipment, libraries, institutions, and structured lending workflows.
fit: Very strong conceptual fit for Hylono rental/access programs. It is materially closer to Hylono’s lending/rental reality than generic IT asset tools.
runtime: Rails-based application stack with database, background jobs, mailers, and operational workflows around reservations and lending.
components: Web app, relational database, workers/jobs, mail, storage, and role-based operational UI.
deploy: The leihs-instance repo is the most useful public deployment blueprint; production still requires standard app hardening, storage, backups, and role design.
deps: Database, mailer, storage, background jobs, reverse proxy/TLS, and operational process configuration.
datastores: Relational database plus file/storage components.
auth: Application roles and authentication; verify current SSO integration options if required.
apis: Operational web UI and app interfaces; API surface should be verified against current version before integration assumptions.
security: Reservation histories and user assignments may include personal data. Access control and auditability matter for rental operations.
setup_path: Begin with leihs-instance and the main repo docs. Validate whether its workflow model matches Hylono rental logistics before deep implementation.
env: App secrets, DB settings, mailer, storage paths, base URLs, worker settings, and any identity/SMTP integrations.
persistence: Back up database and uploaded files; treat reservations and lending records as operational truth if adopted.
operations: User role mapping, reservation policy tuning, upgrade testing, and reporting/export checks.
failure_points: Underestimating workflow adaptation, ignoring customer-facing UX requirements, and assuming institutional defaults match premium commercial rental operations.
integration: Strong candidate to back rental/access programs, equipment availability, reservation states, and handoff/return operations. Could integrate with Next.js booking flows, CRM, notifications, and billing.
verify: Confirm current API/integration surface, multilingual/customer-facing UX suitability, and whether Hylono wants Leihs as the rental source of truth or only as the back-office engine.
risk: Moderate. Best functional fit for rentals, but likely needs adaptation work and careful UX/process mapping.
gdpr: Contains identifiable booking and equipment assignment data; EU hosting is a positive fit if deployed carefully.
overlap: Preferred over Snipe-IT for lending/rental. Could overlap partly with custom booking flows or Fleetbase logistics later.
codex_read_first: Read the main repo and leihs-instance deployment blueprint before proposing rental-system design.
codex_best_path: Prototype Leihs as a back-office rental engine first. Expose only the customer-facing pieces needed through Hylono’s Next.js site or APIs.
codex_never_assume: Never assume Leihs customer UX is ready for direct exposure without adaptation. Never assume its institutional workflow defaults match commercial rental needs.

name: Cal.com
what: Open-source scheduling platform for appointment booking, calendar sync, availability, and event-based booking flows.
solves: Booking pages, staff/resource availability, scheduling workflows, and customer self-service appointment selection.
users: Sales, support, clinics, service teams, and operators who need flexible booking flows.
fit: High fit for consultations, onboarding calls, service bookings, demos, and potentially rental/support scheduling across the Hylono site.
runtime: Next.js/Node-based application stack with PostgreSQL, Prisma, background jobs, integrations, and calendar providers.
components: Web app, DB, cron/jobs, calendar integrations, emails/webhooks, and optional embed flows.
deploy: Self-host via official docs with PostgreSQL and scheduled jobs; production needs calendar provider setup, reverse proxy, and reliability on webhooks/cron.
deps: PostgreSQL, Node runtime, cron/background jobs, mail, calendar provider credentials, and OAuth/app credentials for integrations.
datastores: PostgreSQL primary datastore.
auth: Local/workspace auth plus calendar/provider auth and optional SSO paths depending on plan/setup.
apis: Booking APIs, webhooks, embeds, OAuth/provider integrations, and scheduling/event workflows.
security: Bookings can expose personal contact details and availability data. Harden admin/user access and validate webhook/auth flows carefully.
setup_path: Use official self-host docs. Validate booking flows, availability rules, and calendar sync before public rollout.
env: DATABASE_URL, NEXTAUTH/APP secrets, calendar provider creds, email config, base URLs, cron settings, and webhook endpoints.
persistence: Back up PostgreSQL and preserve booking/event configurations.
operations: Cron reliability, calendar webhook delivery, timezone correctness, and provider API quota/permission review.
failure_points: Broken calendar sync, timezone mistakes, and over-embedding without testing SSR/security behavior in the main site.
integration: Excellent fit with Next.js booking flows, Novu reminders, n8n orchestration, Twenty CRM lead creation, Stripe-paid sessions if needed, and analytics.
verify: Clarify whether Hylono wants Cal.com as the booking source of truth or just as an embedded scheduling surface fronting internal workflows.
risk: Moderate. High user-facing value, but calendar sync and scheduling edge cases create support load.
gdpr: Stores identifiable booking data and calendar context. EU hosting, retention, and third-party calendar provider handling matter.
overlap: Could overlap partly with custom booking flows in Next.js, but Cal.com is stronger for availability/scheduling logic.
codex_read_first: Read official self-host docs and map all current Hylono booking routes and staff/resource needs.
codex_best_path: Use Cal.com for scheduling logic and embed/integrate it into the server-first Next.js site rather than rebuilding calendars internally.
codex_never_assume: Never assume provider sync, recurring availability, or timezone behavior without environment-specific testing.

name: Documenso
license: Open core / verify current repo and enterprise split before bundling code
what: Open-source-first e-signature platform for signing workflows, document templates, recipients, and audit trails.
solves: Digital agreements, waivers, contracts, consents, and internal approvals with a self-hosted or controlled deployment model.
users: Operations, legal, HR, and customer-facing teams that need signatures without depending entirely on closed SaaS.
fit: High fit for waivers, rental agreements, acknowledgements, and premium service documents—provided Hylono carefully defines what is commercial, legal, or health-adjacent.
runtime: Web app with database, storage, mail, document processing, and signing/audit workflow layers.
components: Frontend/API, database, file storage, mailer, signing workflow engine, and enterprise add-ons depending on use case.
deploy: Official Docker and Docker Compose options are available; production requires external database, file storage, and mail configuration.
deps: PostgreSQL or supported DB, object/file storage, mailer, TLS, and strong secret management.
datastores: Relational database plus document/file storage.
auth: Local auth and enterprise/SSO options depending on edition; verify if ZITADEL SSO is required.
apis: Document/template APIs, signing workflows, webhooks, and embed options depending on edition/features.
security: Signed documents, audit trails, and potentially sensitive acknowledgements make this a high-trust system. Protect storage, links, identities, and retention policies carefully.
setup_path: Start with the official Docker Compose path; validate document retention, audit logs, and legal sufficiency before production rollout.
env: DB URL, NEXTAUTH/app secrets, storage credentials, mail config, app URLs, and enterprise license key if applicable.
persistence: Back up database and document storage together; test integrity of audit records and signed artifacts.
operations: Version upgrades, storage migration, email deliverability, and document retention/governance need explicit ownership.
failure_points: Assuming 'open-source e-sign' automatically equals legal sufficiency for every workflow, or mixing signed-doc truth across multiple systems.
integration: Strong fit with Next.js rental/booking flows, Stripe checkout follow-ups, Novu notifications, n8n orchestration, CRM events, and storage backends.
verify: Classify each Hylono signing use case: marketing consent, rental agreement, waiver, acknowledgment, internal approval. Verify legal requirements per jurisdiction/use case.
risk: Moderate to high because signature workflows carry legal/process consequences.
gdpr: Documents may contain identity, addresses, signatures, and possibly health-adjacent acknowledgements. EU hosting and retention controls are important.
overlap: Overlaps with DocuSeal. Documenso is the stronger current candidate for Hylono if a more mature, EU-friendly signature workflow is desired.
codex_read_first: Read self-host docs and define document classes and legal expectations before integrating anything into the site.
codex_best_path: Use Documenso for clearly bounded document workflows first (for example rental agreements or acknowledgements), and keep signed artifacts in controlled storage.
codex_never_assume: Never assume a signature workflow is legally or operationally sufficient without human validation of the exact document use case.

name: ZITADEL
researched_as: ZITADEL identity and access management platform
license: Apache-2.0 / verify current distribution terms if enterprise features are considered
what: Identity and access management platform with SSO, MFA, passkeys, OIDC, SAML, SCIM, and organization/project models.
solves: Centralized authentication, SSO, machine identity, tenant/org identity management, and standards-based auth brokering across applications.
users: Engineering and platform teams that want a self-hostable identity provider rather than stitching auth separately into every internal tool.
fit: Excellent fit. Hylono already runs Auth.js on the site, and ZITADEL is the strongest self-hosted candidate in this stack for cross-app SSO, machine identities, and externalized identity governance.
runtime: Go-based IAM platform with database, admin console, auth endpoints, and standards-based identity protocols.
components: Identity service, console, database, TLS endpoints, email/SMS/provider integrations, and optional Helm/Kubernetes deployment artifacts.
deploy: Official Docker Compose exists for evaluation, but official docs recommend Kubernetes/Helm for production-scale self-hosting.
deps: Database, TLS/reverse proxy, mail/SMS providers, secrets management, backup/restore, and identity-provider configuration.
datastores: Relational database plus config/secrets and optional external providers.
auth: It is the auth layer: OIDC, OAuth2, SAML, SCIM, MFA, passkeys, service users, and organization/project-scoped controls.
apis: OIDC/OAuth2 endpoints, management APIs, SCIM, SAML, and admin/automation interfaces.
security: This is a tier-0 identity system. Key management, domain/TLS correctness, email/SMS trust chains, and upgrade discipline are critical.
setup_path: Use official self-host docs; start with evaluation on Docker only if needed, but target production architecture around the official Helm/Kubernetes path.
env: DB settings, external domain, TLS, machine keys, SMTP, SMS/IDP configs, org/project setup, and backup strategy.
persistence: Back up the database and any relevant keys/config; identity recovery planning matters.
operations: Version upgrades, OIDC client management, key rotation, domain verification, email deliverability, and incident response preparedness.
failure_points: Treating identity as a side project, underestimating migration from existing Auth.js/NextAuth flows, and misconfiguring redirect URIs or external domains.
integration: High-value integration with Next.js/Auth.js as an external IdP, internal tool SSO (Twenty, Appsmith, Metabase, BookStack, etc.), Kong/OIDC, and machine-to-machine auth patterns.
verify: Decide whether Hylono wants ZITADEL to become the central IdP now, or whether Auth.js remains primary for the site while ZITADEL covers internal tools first.
risk: High consequence but high leverage. Worth doing carefully and early if cross-app SSO is a priority.
gdpr: Identity data is personal data. EU hosting, auditability, retention, and breach response are central.
overlap: Could overlap with app-local auth systems and portions of Auth.js. Recommended as the primary self-hosted IdP rather than many disconnected auth stacks.
codex_read_first: Read official self-host docs, Auth.js provider strategy, and the current Hylono auth architecture before proposing migration or brokering.
codex_best_path: Introduce ZITADEL first as the IdP for internal tools and selected admin surfaces, then decide whether the public site should federate through it.
codex_never_assume: Never assume identity migration is low-risk. Never assume every app supports the same standards or claims mapping without testing.

name: n8n
license: Sustainable Use License / fair-code style; review before bundling or redistribution
what: Workflow automation platform for API orchestration, webhooks, ETL-style workflows, integrations, and agentic automations.
solves: Low-code/high-flexibility automation across webhooks, SaaS APIs, internal apps, queues, and scheduled tasks without hardcoding every integration into the site.
users: Ops, growth, engineering, and integration teams that need fast automation and event choreography.
fit: Very high fit. Hylono already cares about n8n integration, and it is the natural glue between Next.js APIs, Twenty, Novu, Stripe, Cal.com, documents, and internal admin tooling.
runtime: Node.js automation platform with workflow engine, database, workers/queue mode, webhook ingestion, and credentials store.
components: Editor/UI, execution engine, DB, optional queue workers, Redis for queue mode, webhooks, credentials store, and integrations/nodes.
deploy: Docker is standard; production may use queue mode with Redis, separate workers, persistent database, and public webhook reachability.
deps: Database, optional Redis for queue mode, webhook ingress, mail/notifications, secrets handling, and storage for binary data if used.
datastores: Database for workflows/executions plus optional Redis for queue mode.
auth: Built-in user auth, API keys, credential vaulting inside n8n, and webhook auth patterns.
apis: REST API, webhook triggers, MCP trigger, hundreds of nodes/connectors, and import/export of workflows.
security: Extremely powerful blast radius. Protect credentials, isolate environments, restrict who can edit workflows, and avoid sensitive payload sprawl in executions.
setup_path: Use official self-host docs; deploy with persistent DB, reverse proxy/TLS, and public webhook routing. Add queue mode only when concurrency or reliability requires it.
env: DB settings, encryption key, webhook URL, host/base URL, Redis settings if queue mode, execution retention, and credential secrets.
persistence: Back up database and export important workflows; review binary data retention and execution logs.
operations: Webhook delivery, failed execution handling, credential rotation, workflow versioning, and worker scaling.
failure_points: Using it as an ungoverned shadow backend, leaking secrets into workflow nodes, and failing to test webhook callbacks end to end.
integration: First-class fit with Next.js routes, Stripe, Resend, Novu, Twenty, Cal.com, Documenso, Lago, PostHog, Slack/internal alerts, and future AI/ops flows.
verify: Define clear system boundaries: which automations stay in n8n versus in application code. Decide who owns workflow review and credential governance.
risk: Moderate. Huge leverage, but it can create invisible technical debt if not governed.
gdpr: Workflow payloads can capture personal data. Apply retention controls, redact where possible, and choose EU-hosted deployment.
overlap: Overlaps with Node-RED for automation; n8n is the better default for business/system workflows in this stack.
codex_read_first: Read current Next.js API routes, webhook/event map, and n8n production docs before building flows.
codex_best_path: Use n8n as the integration/orchestration layer around the core app, not as a substitute for domain logic that belongs in the app codebase.
codex_never_assume: Never assume local-test webhooks or tunnel behavior represent production. Never assume execution data can be retained indefinitely.

components: Web app/API, relational database, mailer, payment connectors, cron/jobs, and PDF/document generation.

datastores: Relational database plus generated invoice/document storage.
auth: Local auth, client portal users, API tokens, and payment-provider credentials.

datastores: Relational database.
auth: Local user roles; verify SSO options if needed.
apis: Accounting/invoice/bank-related app surfaces; deeper API suitability should be tested against current version.
security: Financial and accounting records create governance obligations beyond ordinary app data.

overlap: Substantial overlap with Invoice Ninja and external accounting suites.

name: Node-RED
what: Flow-based programming and automation platform with strong IoT, MQTT, and message-routing heritage.

users: IoT, OT, and integration teams that need visual event/message flows.
fit: Useful later if Hylono builds device telemetry or MQTT-heavy workflows. For current business/process automation, n8n is the better default.
runtime: Node.js runtime hosting flows, connectors, dashboards (if used), and integration nodes.
components: Editor/runtime, flows, nodes/plugins, optional dashboard/UI, and persistence for flow definitions.
deploy: Docker or simple service deployment is straightforward. Governance matters more than install.
deps: Persistent storage for flows, broker/API endpoints, optional MQTT, and plugin/node governance.
datastores: Local flow storage or attached storage; optional external systems for state.
auth: Admin auth, node credentials, and network/proxy protection.

env: Editor/base URL, auth, storage path, node credentials, broker settings, and reverse-proxy config.

operations: Node/plugin review, flow testing, and environment separation matter.

name: Infisical

what: Secrets management platform for application secrets, machine identity, secret sync, and environment configuration.

components: Control plane/UI, database, encryption/key management, CLI/SDK, secret sync/injection, and policy/audit layers.
deploy: Self-host docs are mature; production requires reliable DB, key management, backup, and role design.

codex_best_path: Introduce Infisical early for shared infrastructure and integration services, then gradually move app secrets under policy control.
