overlap: Partial overlap with Fides and internal docs/evidence systems, but different focus.
codex_read_first: Read the compliance strategy notes before recommending Comp AI.
codex_best_path: Only implement when there are named owners, frameworks, and evidence workflows to support.
codex_never_assume: Never assume software creates a compliance program by itself.

name: PostHog
version: Current self-host docs reviewed; exact monorepo server version intentionally left UNVERIFIED
version_status: CONFIRMED product state / exact version UNVERIFIED
license: MIT / open-core mix depending on components; verify current packaging as needed
what: Product analytics, feature flags, session replay, experiments, and data warehouse-ish product instrumentation platform.
solves: Event analytics, funnels, experiments, feature flags, user behavior analysis, and product instrumentation.
users: Product, growth, and engineering teams.
fit: Already in Hylono’s stack and high value. The key question is not adoption but deployment posture: official guidance still positions self-hosting as more advanced/beta while cloud is preferred.
runtime: Large multi-service application with ClickHouse, PostgreSQL, Redis, object storage, workers/plugins, and optional extensive event ingestion.
components: Web app, plugin server, event ingestion, ClickHouse, PostgreSQL, Redis, object storage, workers, and analytics features.
deploy: Operationally heavy self-hosting. Official docs emphasize hobby/beta self-host paths and recommend cloud for most users.
deps: ClickHouse, PostgreSQL, Redis, object storage, background workers, secure ingress, and capacity planning.
datastores: ClickHouse, PostgreSQL, Redis, object storage.
auth: Built-in auth/org/project model plus data access controls and API keys.
apis: Analytics SDKs, APIs, feature flags, data exports, and plugin/event surfaces.
security: Event pipelines can capture sensitive product and customer context. Data minimization and retention are essential.
setup_path: If Hylono already uses PostHog cloud and that satisfies EU/data requirements, keeping it managed is likely simpler than self-hosting.
env: Multiservice infra config, object storage, databases, secrets, SDK keys, and worker settings.
persistence: All backing stores need coordinated backup strategy in self-hosted mode.
operations: Event volume, cost/capacity, data retention, schema drift, and privacy controls dominate.
failure_points: Self-hosting for control without resourcing the full data/ops burden, or duplicating analytics across multiple tools.
integration: Already integrated with the Next.js site. Also useful for feature flags, lifecycle analytics, and internal admin/product instrumentation.
verify: Confirm whether current managed PostHog already satisfies EU/data and feature requirements. If yes, do not replatform lightly.
risk: Moderate if managed; high if self-hosted.
gdpr: Analytics data is personal data when identifiable. IP/session replay/feature flags all require careful governance.
recommendation: KEEP CURRENT USE; AVOID SELF-HOST REPLATFORM UNLESS REQUIRED
overlap: Some overlap with Formbricks for feedback and with Metabase for reporting, but PostHog remains the product analytics anchor.
codex_read_first: Read the existing PostHog integration in the Hylono site before proposing any changes.
codex_best_path: Keep PostHog focused on product analytics and feature flags; avoid self-hosting unless strong data-control reasons justify the heavier platform footprint.
codex_never_assume: Never assume self-host PostHog is an easy drop-in just because the code is open.

name: Retraced
what: Developer-friendly audit logging service for recording and querying security- or compliance-relevant events.
solves: Centralizing audit trails separate from application code, especially for customer-facing admin actions or compliance-sensitive workflows.
users: Engineering and security teams that need structured audit logging.
fit: Useful if Hylono wants centralized audit logs across internal/admin surfaces, but not a first-wave need unless compliance expectations rise.
runtime: Service/API with storage backend and query/report surfaces.
components: Audit ingestion API, storage, query UI/APIs, and deployment manifests including Kubernetes references.
deploy: Can be self-hosted, but project cadence looks quieter than some alternatives. Maturity and maintenance should be revalidated before adoption.
deps: Database/storage, API ingress, app instrumentation, and auth/access controls.
datastores: Relational or attached storage depending on deployment.
auth: Admin/service auth and API keys.
apis: Audit log ingestion and query APIs.
security: Audit logs are sensitive and can themselves contain personal or security-relevant details.
setup_path: Only prioritize if Hylono has a real audit-log centralization requirement beyond app-native audit features.
env: Storage/DB config, service secrets, API auth, base URL, and retention settings.
persistence: Audit log retention and tamper-resistance expectations should be decided up front.
operations: Instrumentation coverage, retention, and access review matter most.
failure_points: Adding a separate audit system without actually instrumenting the important actions.
integration: Could receive audit events from Next.js admin paths, ZITADEL/Kong, Appsmith, and internal tools if central audit becomes important.
verify: Revalidate project maintenance cadence before implementation.
risk: Moderate maturity risk.
gdpr: Audit logs can contain identifiers; retention and access must be controlled.
overlap: Partial overlap with app-native audit features and security platforms.
codex_read_first: Read current audit requirements and verify project activity before proceeding.
codex_best_path: Use only if centralized audit logging is a confirmed requirement and existing app logs are insufficient.
codex_never_assume: Never assume central audit logging is valuable if critical actions are not instrumented consistently.

assets.name: Official repo
assets.notes: Primary code source; cadence should be reviewed.

name: Fides
researched_as: Fides privacy and data governance platform
license: Apache-2.0 / verify enterprise/commercial layers if used
what: Open-source privacy engineering and data governance platform for consent, privacy requests, data inventory, and policy enforcement workflows.
solves: Operationalizing privacy programs: consent, DSAR workflows, data mapping, and governance over data systems.
users: Privacy, legal, security, and engineering teams with active governance requirements.
fit: Potentially valuable for GDPR maturity, but heavier than CookieConsent or Presidio because it is a program-level privacy platform rather than a single feature component.
runtime: Containerized multi-service platform with database, web app, workers, and integrations to data systems.
components: Privacy center/admin UI, database, consent/request workflows, data connectors, and orchestration services.
deploy: Production docs exist for modern container orchestration. This is a real platform, not a lightweight plugin.
deps: Database, workers, mail, app/data-system connectors, secrets management, and privacy-process ownership.
datastores: Relational database plus connector metadata.
auth: Admin/user roles and auth integrations depending on deployment.
apis: Consent/privacy APIs, connectors, request orchestration, and governance interfaces.
security: Handles sensitive privacy inventory and request workflows. Needs careful access control and process ownership.
setup_path: Only prioritize once Hylono commits to a real privacy program beyond basic cookie banners and policy docs.
env: DB settings, app secrets, connector configs, mail, auth, and deployment-specific worker settings.
persistence: Back up the DB and preserve privacy request/state integrity.
operations: Connector maintenance, privacy workflow ownership, legal/policy updates, and access review are ongoing.
failure_points: Deploying a governance platform before the organization has clear privacy processes and data maps.
integration: Could eventually integrate with site consent flows, DSAR processes, data systems, and engineering controls.
verify: Clarify whether Hylono currently needs programmatic GDPR/consent orchestration or only basic consent management plus disciplined data handling.
risk: Moderate to high process burden.
gdpr: Directly relevant. Strong tool only if the privacy program is ready to use it.
overlap: Different layer than CookieConsent or Presidio; broader privacy operations platform.
codex_read_first: Read actual privacy-program requirements before suggesting Fides implementation.
codex_best_path: Keep Fides on the roadmap for later governance maturity, not as an immediate deployment item.
codex_never_assume: Never assume privacy software is useful without process and ownership behind it.

name: CookieConsent
what: Lightweight cookie consent and script-blocking library for websites.
solves: Cookie banners, consent categories, script gating, and front-end consent UX.
users: Web teams that need basic consent management without a heavy CMP platform.
fit: Good tactical fit for the Hylono website if current consent UX needs improvement. It is not a full privacy program tool, but it is useful and low-friction.
runtime: Frontend JavaScript library integrated into the website.
components: Banner, modal/preferences UI, consent state handling, script category control, and translation/config options.
deploy: Very light. Integrates directly into the existing Next.js frontend.
deps: Frontend integration and legal/policy decisions about categories/copy.
datastores: Browser-side consent state plus any server-side recording Hylono adds.
auth: None.
apis: Frontend config/hooks and consent events.
security: Main concern is legal/config correctness, not infrastructure.
setup_path: Integrate directly into the Next.js site if a lightweight consent banner is sufficient.
env: Frontend config values and category definitions.
persistence: Consent state in browser or custom server-side logs if implemented.
operations: Copy/category maintenance and QA after site changes.
failure_points: Treating a banner library as full GDPR compliance.
integration: Fits neatly into the existing Next.js frontend and analytics script loading.
verify: Confirm whether Hylono needs only a banner/script-control layer or a fuller CMP/privacy operations stack.
risk: Low.
gdpr: Relevant for consent UX, but only one piece of compliance.
overlap: Different layer than Fides or Presidio.
codex_read_first: Read the current site analytics/consent implementation before changing anything.
codex_best_path: Implement as a focused front-end consent component if existing consent UX is weak.
codex_never_assume: Never treat a cookie banner as complete compliance coverage.

name: Kong
license: Apache-2.0 for open-source gateway / enterprise layers available
what: API gateway for routing, auth, rate limiting, plugins, observability, and API policy enforcement.
solves: Central API ingress, OIDC/JWT auth, rate limiting, service routing, plugin-based controls, and boundary hardening for many services.
users: Platform and API teams operating multiple services and integrations.
fit: Excellent fit. Hylono already has multiple APIs and self-hosted services in scope; Kong is the cleanest traffic/control-plane anchor for a growing internal/external service estate.
runtime: Nginx/OpenResty-based gateway with PostgreSQL-backed config or DB-less mode plus plugin ecosystem.
components: Gateway nodes, config store/DB, plugins, admin APIs, declarative config, and observability outputs.
deploy: Docker Compose or Kubernetes are common. PostgreSQL-backed mode is practical when plugin/state features are needed.
deps: PostgreSQL for DB-backed mode, reverse proxy/TLS, plugin config, monitoring, and careful route/policy design.
datastores: PostgreSQL in DB-backed mode, or declarative config in DB-less mode.
auth: OIDC, JWT, key auth, ACLs, mTLS, and many plugin-based patterns.
apis: Admin API, gateway routes/services/plugins, declarative config, metrics, and plugin ecosystem.
security: High leverage security boundary. Misconfiguration can expose services or break auth globally.
setup_path: Adopt early for service governance if the stack expands beyond a few isolated apps. Pair with ZITADEL for IdP and Infisical for secrets.
env: Kong DB settings or declarative config, proxy/admin ports, TLS, plugin secrets, and service upstream definitions.
persistence: If DB-backed, back up Kong PostgreSQL; if declarative, version control the config.
operations: Route/plugin testing, certificate handling, upgrade compatibility, and metrics/logging need ownership.
failure_points: Overcomplicating a very small stack, or deploying Kong without a clear API catalog and route ownership model.
integration: Strong central fit for Next.js APIs, internal tools, webhooks, ZITADEL/OIDC, rate limits, service discovery, and observability.
verify: Decide whether Kong fronts only internal admin/service traffic or also public Hylono APIs.
risk: Moderate but foundationally useful.
gdpr: Gateway logs may include identifiers; configure logging carefully.
overlap: Some overlap with ad hoc reverse proxies, but Kong becomes the intentional API boundary.
codex_read_first: Read the dependency map and current API surface before designing Kong routes.
codex_best_path: Introduce Kong as the API boundary for internal/self-hosted services first, then selectively front public APIs where policy controls matter.
codex_never_assume: Never expose an admin plane publicly. Never add gateway complexity without a clear service-boundary plan.

name: Prometheus
what: Metrics collection, storage, alerting, and query platform for infrastructure and application observability.
solves: Infrastructure/app metrics, alerting rules, and time-series visibility across self-hosted services.
users: Ops and engineering teams running services that need real observability.
fit: Strong fit. Hylono’s stack is large enough that a real metrics layer is justified, especially if self-hosted apps grow.
runtime: Single binary/server with TSDB, scrape configs, service discovery, and alert rules.
components: Prometheus server, exporters, alerting rules, and usually Alertmanager/Grafana around it.
deploy: Straightforward container/Kubernetes deployment. Design scrape targets and retention deliberately.
deps: Persistent storage, exporters, service discovery config, and alert routing.
datastores: Prometheus local TSDB plus remote-write options if needed.
auth: Usually protected behind network/reverse proxy; core Prometheus auth is limited.
apis: PromQL, HTTP APIs, scrape endpoints, federation, and remote write/read.
security: Metrics can expose internal topology and sensitive labels; protect endpoints and sanitize labels.
setup_path: Deploy alongside Grafana and Alertmanager when more than lightweight uptime checks are needed.
env: Scrape configs, retention, storage path, alert configs, and exporter endpoints.
persistence: Back up configuration; TSDB backups depend on retention needs and remote-write strategy.
operations: Target discovery, alert tuning, storage sizing, and metrics cardinality control matter.
failure_points: Unbounded cardinality, poor alert hygiene, and exposing metrics publicly.
integration: Backbone for monitoring databases, Kong, app services, n8n, Novu, and infrastructure.
verify: Decide whether a simple initial stack is Prometheus + Grafana + exporters + Uptime Kuma, and which services are tier-0.
risk: Low to moderate; essential as the stack grows.
gdpr: Metrics should avoid personal data labels.
overlap: Complements Uptime Kuma rather than replacing it.
codex_read_first: Read the service inventory and identify exporters/metrics endpoints first.
codex_best_path: Roll out Prometheus with a small curated target set and pair it with Grafana.
codex_never_assume: Never scrape everything indiscriminately or allow high-cardinality labels to explode costs.

name: Metabase
what: Business intelligence and dashboarding platform for querying databases, building charts, and sharing internal analytics.
solves: Self-service reporting for operations, sales, bookings, rentals, finance, and product analytics outside product-embedded dashboards.
users: Operators, analysts, finance, and business teams who need database-backed dashboards.
fit: Strong fit for internal business analytics across Hylono’s growing operational systems.
runtime: Java application with metadata DB and connections to source databases/warehouses.
components: Web app, metadata DB, source DB connectors, dashboard/query layer, and sharing/permissions.
deploy: Self-host is mature; production needs metadata DB, SSO decisions, and data-source governance.
deps: Application runtime, metadata DB, source DB connectivity, reverse proxy, and optional SSO.
datastores: Own metadata DB plus connected source DBs (Postgres, etc.).
auth: Local auth and SSO options depending on edition/setup.
apis: REST APIs, embedding options, SQL/native query tools, and dashboard sharing.
security: Direct database access and shared dashboards require careful permissions and row/table exposure decisions.
setup_path: Deploy after key operational data sources stabilize; expose curated dashboards rather than raw DB access everywhere.
env: MB_DB settings, source DB credentials, app URL, mail, auth config, and embedding settings.
persistence: Back up metadata DB and version key dashboard definitions as needed.
operations: Upgrade metadata DB carefully, manage source permissions, and avoid uncontrolled analyst-level DB access.
failure_points: Connecting it to production databases with overly broad privileges, or using it to paper over poor source-of-truth design.
integration: Excellent for reporting on CRM, bookings, billing, rental operations, and operational KPIs. Pair with Twenty, Lago, Postgres, and other app DBs.
verify: Decide which source systems become analytics sources and whether derived reporting DBs are needed later.
risk: Moderate; high value if permissions are managed well.
gdpr: Dashboards may expose identifiable data; role-based access and minimization matter.
overlap: Some overlap with PostHog for product analytics, but Metabase is broader business reporting.
codex_read_first: Read the source-of-truth map before connecting Metabase to everything.
codex_best_path: Start with a small curated set of operational dashboards over trusted Postgres sources.
codex_never_assume: Never grant Metabase unrestricted production DB access by default.

name: Appsmith
what: Low-code platform for building internal tools, dashboards, CRUD apps, and operator consoles.
solves: Rapid internal tooling without shipping every admin surface into the customer-facing Next.js codebase.
users: Ops, support, engineering, and business teams needing internal interfaces over data and APIs.
fit: Strong fit. Hylono will likely need internal admin/ops tools, and Appsmith can absorb that need without bloating the public site.
runtime: Containerized web platform with database connections, JS widgets, APIs, and auth/user management.
components: Builder UI, runtime, DB/API connectors, user/workspace model, and deployment services.
deploy: Docker Compose or Kubernetes are supported. Production requires SSO, environment promotion, and data-connector discipline.
deps: Persistent storage, Appsmith runtime, auth/SSO, database/API credentials, and reverse proxy.
datastores: Internal metadata store plus connected data sources/APIs.
auth: Built-in auth with SSO/OIDC/SAML options depending on setup.
apis: Connectors to SQL/REST/GraphQL and JS-based internal apps.
security: Internal tooling can become a privileged data surface. Restrict connectors, credentials, and publishing/admin rights carefully.
setup_path: Good candidate after identity/secrets/gateway layers are in place. Use it for internal ops, not customer-facing pages.
env: App URL, auth settings, storage, encryption/secrets, and connector credentials.
persistence: Back up metadata/config and version critical apps where possible.
operations: Connector governance, environment separation, and role/access review matter.
failure_points: Letting Appsmith become an uncontrolled privileged UI over every production system.
integration: Excellent for internal CRM/rental/ops dashboards over Twenty, Postgres, APIs, and workflow systems.
verify: Decide which internal tools should live in Appsmith versus custom Next.js admin surfaces.
risk: Moderate but very useful if governed.
gdpr: Can expose personal data from many systems, so permissioning is key.
overlap: Overlaps with building custom internal admin UIs in Next.js; Appsmith is faster for many internal ops surfaces.
codex_read_first: Read the internal-tooling needs and current admin burden before scoping Appsmith.
codex_best_path: Use Appsmith for internal-only CRUD/ops tools where speed matters more than pixel-perfect custom UX.
codex_never_assume: Never expose Appsmith publicly or connect it to production systems with overly broad credentials.

name: ThingsBoard
what: IoT platform for device connectivity, telemetry ingestion, rules, dashboards, and device management.
solves: Managing connected devices, telemetry, alarms, dashboards, and device-side integrations.
users: IoT and operations teams running connected devices or sensors.
fit: Potentially valuable if Hylono expands into device telemetry or connected equipment operations. Not necessary for a protocol-first business unless device connectivity becomes core.
runtime: Java/microservice-style platform with DB, message broker options, telemetry ingestion, and dashboards.
components: Transport/connectivity layer, rule engine, dashboards, device registry, and storage integrations.
deploy: Self-hosting is mature but operationally meaningful. Production architecture should match actual device scale.
deps: Databases, brokers/queues, storage, and device/MQTT connectivity.
datastores: PostgreSQL/Cassandra/Timeseries integrations depending on setup; verify chosen architecture carefully.
auth: Users, tenants, customers, and device credentials/tokens.
apis: MQTT/HTTP/CoAP device APIs, REST APIs, dashboards, and rule-chain integrations.
security: Connected devices and telemetry can create large attack surface and sensitive operational data exposure.
setup_path: Only implement when Hylono has real connected-device requirements beyond simple monitoring.
env: DB/broker settings, transport config, auth/device credential settings, and cluster sizing.
persistence: Depends on telemetry/storage design; backups must include config and metadata.
operations: Device lifecycle, telemetry volume, rule-chain correctness, and multi-tenant data separation matter.
failure_points: Deploying a full IoT platform before confirming device telemetry is a strategic requirement.
integration: Strong only if Hylono products/devices become connected and need dashboards/alerts.
verify: Confirm whether connected-device telemetry is a near-term roadmap item.
risk: High complexity with uncertain immediate value.
gdpr: Telemetry and customer-device linkage can become personal data.
recommendation: LATER / ONLY IF IOT STRATEGY IS REAL
overlap: Could overlap with Node-RED + Mosquitto + InfluxDB/Grafana depending on sophistication.
codex_read_first: Read the IoT strategy and overlap map before touching ThingsBoard.
codex_best_path: Defer until device telemetry is real and architectural requirements are clear.
codex_never_assume: Never deploy an IoT platform just because device-adjacent business exists.

name: Mosquitto
what: Lightweight MQTT broker for device messaging and pub/sub communication.
solves: MQTT transport for IoT devices, local brokers, event buses, and low-overhead message routing.
users: IoT, OT, and integration teams.
fit: Useful only if Hylono’s device or telemetry roadmap requires MQTT. Not needed for ordinary web/business workflows.
runtime: Single broker service with ACLs, bridges, persistence, and TLS.
components: Broker, config, persistence store, ACLs/auth, and optional bridges.
deploy: Easy to self-host; governance comes from what devices/flows depend on it.
deps: Persistent storage if durable sessions/messages matter, TLS certs, auth, and client governance.
datastores: Broker persistence files if enabled.
auth: Passwords, ACLs, certificates, and plugin-based auth patterns.
apis: MQTT protocol and broker admin/config surfaces.
security: Broker exposure is risky. Use TLS, ACLs, and network isolation.
setup_path: Deploy only as part of a confirmed MQTT/device architecture, likely with Node-RED/ThingsBoard/InfluxDB.
env: Listener ports, persistence, ACLs, TLS certs, bridge config, and auth files.
persistence: Back up config and persistence store if using durable state.
operations: Client ACLs, connection monitoring, TLS, and broker uptime matter.
failure_points: Introducing MQTT infrastructure without an actual MQTT use case.
integration: Best used with device/telemetry tooling, not core business apps.
verify: Confirm actual MQTT/device needs first.
risk: Low infrastructure risk, high unnecessary-complexity risk if premature.
gdpr: Depends on device payloads.
overlap: Part of the IoT stack; not an overlap with n8n/HTTP systems.
codex_read_first: Read the IoT notes before considering Mosquitto.
codex_best_path: Add only when a real MQTT topology is defined.
codex_never_assume: Never deploy brokers without clear client and ACL design.

name: InfluxDB
license: MIT/Apache mix depending on components; verify current packaging
what: Time-series database for metrics, events, and telemetry workloads.
solves: High-cardinality time-series storage, device telemetry, and operational event analytics.
users: IoT, observability, and analytics teams.
fit: Useful only if Hylono needs device telemetry or a distinct time-series store beyond Prometheus/ClickHouse/Postgres.
runtime: Time-series database server with query engines and data ingest paths.
components: DB engine, storage, query/API layer, and ingestion endpoints.
deploy: Self-host is feasible; choose only when a clear timeseries need exists.
deps: Persistent storage, ingest clients, and query/retention planning.
datastores: InfluxDB itself.
auth: Tokens/users/org/bucket access controls.
apis: Write APIs, query APIs, CLI, and integrations.
security: Protect telemetry and auth tokens; retention and bucket policies matter.
setup_path: Do not prioritize until time-series needs exceed Prometheus or simpler stores.
env: Storage, org/bucket, tokens, retention, and network/TLS settings.
persistence: Back up bucket/data as required; retention design is central.
operations: Cardinality, retention, storage, and query costs matter.
failure_points: Adding a separate timeseries database before proving its necessity.
integration: Useful with device telemetry, Grafana, and IoT stack; not a first-wave business-system dependency.
verify: Clarify whether Prometheus alone covers observability and whether device telemetry is near-term.
risk: Moderate unnecessary-complexity risk.
gdpr: Depends on telemetry content and identifiers.
recommendation: LATER / IF TIME-SERIES NEEDS BECOME REAL
overlap: May overlap with Prometheus or Postgres-based event storage for smaller workloads.
codex_read_first: Read the observability and IoT stack notes before considering InfluxDB.
codex_best_path: Avoid until a specific telemetry workload justifies it.
codex_never_assume: Never add a dedicated time-series store without a demonstrated query/scale need.

name: Grafana
what: Visualization and observability UI platform for dashboards, alerts, logs, and multiple data sources.
solves: Operational dashboards, metrics visualization, logs/traces integration, and shared visibility across technical systems.
users: Ops, engineering, and technical stakeholders.
fit: Strong fit as the main observability UI once Prometheus and other telemetry sources exist.
runtime: Web application with internal metadata DB and connectors to data sources like Prometheus, Loki, InfluxDB, PostgreSQL, etc.
components: UI, datasource plugins, alerting, dashboards, auth, and metadata store.
deploy: Straightforward self-hosting; usually paired with Prometheus and exporters.
deps: Metadata storage, data-source connectors, auth/SSO, reverse proxy, and alerting config.
datastores: Internal metadata store plus external data sources.
auth: Local auth and SSO/OIDC/SAML options depending on edition/setup.
apis: Dashboard and datasource APIs, plugins, and alerting APIs.
security: Dashboards can expose internal service data. Use RBAC and least-privilege datasource access.
setup_path: Deploy with Prometheus and exporters as part of the observability wave.
env: Admin creds, datasource settings, auth/SSO, SMTP, and storage path.
persistence: Back up Grafana config/DB or provision dashboards as code.
operations: Datasource governance, dashboard sprawl control, and version/plugin upgrades matter.
failure_points: Letting Grafana become a manual dashboard graveyard without provisioning discipline.
integration: Natural fit with Prometheus, Postgres exporters, Kong, Redis, and infrastructure monitoring.
verify: Choose whether dashboards are provisioned as code from day one.
risk: Low to moderate; high value.
gdpr: Avoid exposing identifiable data in dashboards unless role-controlled.
overlap: Complements rather than replaces Prometheus/Uptime Kuma.
codex_read_first: Read observability target list and decide on dashboards-as-code.
codex_best_path: Use Grafana as the shared observability UI over Prometheus and selected app/data sources.
codex_never_assume: Never connect Grafana to production sources with broad privileges without RBAC planning.

name: Meilisearch
what: Fast, developer-friendly full-text search engine for application search and filtering.
solves: Site search, knowledge search, product search, and internal-search UX with typo tolerance and ranking controls.
users: Web and product teams needing search without Elasticsearch-level complexity.
fit: Good fit if Hylono needs strong search across content, products, protocols, or docs.
runtime: Rust service with index storage, REST API, and API keys.
components: Search engine, indexes, API keys, optional sync/indexing pipelines, and front-end clients.
deploy: Operationally light compared with Elasticsearch-class systems.
deps: Persistent storage, indexing pipeline, API key governance, and monitoring.
datastores: Internal Meilisearch indexes.
auth: Master/search/private API keys and tenant tokens.
apis: REST search/index APIs and SDKs.
security: Protect master keys and avoid indexing sensitive internal data into public search endpoints.
setup_path: Good later addition if current site search becomes a real product need.
env: Master key, storage path, env mode, dump/snapshot config, and network settings.
persistence: Use dumps/snapshots and backups of index data or rebuild pipelines.
operations: Reindexing strategy, ranking tuning, and index freshness matter.
failure_points: Indexing the wrong data, stale indexes, or exposing privileged search datasets.
integration: Strong fit for Next.js content/product search and possibly BookStack/Docusaurus search.
verify: Clarify which searchable surfaces matter first: site content, products, docs, or internal ops.
risk: Low to moderate.
gdpr: Search indexes can duplicate personal data if not curated.
recommendation: LATER / HIGH-UTILITY IF SEARCH BECOMES IMPORTANT
overlap: Could replace built-in/site-local search implementations.
codex_read_first: Read actual search requirements and content architecture before implementing.
codex_best_path: Add Meilisearch when there is a clear search surface worth indexing.
codex_never_assume: Never index sensitive datasets into a public-facing search service.

name: Dify
license: Apache-2.0 / commercial cloud offerings also exist
what: LLM application platform for prompt workflows, RAG apps, agents, knowledge bases, and AI app operations.
solves: Building and operating internal or customer-facing AI apps without hand-rolling every orchestration layer.
users: AI/product teams building assistant, knowledge, or agent workflows.
fit: Useful later if Hylono wants AI copilots, internal assistants, or knowledge-grounded workflows. Not necessary just to integrate existing SaaS/web apps.
runtime: Multi-service platform with web app, API, workers, vector/relational stores, object storage, and model-provider integrations.
components: App builder, knowledge base/RAG, workers, model connectors, databases, storage, and API surfaces.
deploy: Self-host quick-start and Helm charts exist, but production adds significant model/vector/storage considerations.
deps: Databases, caches, object storage, model provider keys, possible vector store, and observability.
datastores: Relational DB, caches, storage, and optional vector/database components.
auth: Workspace/user auth and API keys.
apis: LLM app APIs, workflows, knowledge base, and model integration surfaces.
security: Prompt/input data, knowledge bases, and provider keys require strong governance and privacy controls.
setup_path: Only prioritize after core business systems are stable and a real AI product/internal-assistant use case is defined.
env: DB/cache/storage/model settings, app secrets, vector store config, and URLs.
persistence: Back up metadata DB and storage; knowledge bases may be reproducible but ingestion pipelines matter.
operations: Model/provider governance, prompt/version drift, vector/knowledge refresh, and privacy controls matter.
failure_points: Deploying AI infrastructure without defined use cases or data-governance guardrails.
integration: Could later connect to BookStack, Docusaurus, CRM, support, or internal search/PII tooling.
verify: Identify concrete AI use cases and whether Dify is better than lighter custom integrations.
risk: High complexity if used prematurely.
gdpr: AI workflows can handle personal data; minimization and provider review are critical.
overlap: Could overlap with custom AI tooling or n8n-based assistants.
codex_read_first: Read the actual AI use-case notes before considering Dify.
codex_best_path: Defer until there is a clearly scoped internal or customer-facing AI workflow.
codex_never_assume: Never deploy AI platforms just because they are available; start from use case and data policy.

name: Strapi
license: Open-source core with commercial tiers; verify current licensing for enterprise features
what: Headless CMS for structured content modeling, APIs, and editorial workflows.
solves: Editorial content management for websites, knowledge bases, and multi-channel content delivery.
users: Content, marketing, and engineering teams needing a structured CMS.
fit: Conditional. Hylono already has a server-first Next.js content-driven site; Strapi is only worthwhile if current content workflows outgrow in-repo/contentlayer-style approaches or custom admin tooling.
runtime: Node.js CMS with database, admin UI, media library, and REST/GraphQL APIs.
components: Admin UI, content types, APIs, media library, DB, and plugins.
deploy: Mature self-host path. Production requires DB, storage, media handling, auth, and editorial process design.
deps: Database, file storage, reverse proxy, auth/admin security, and backups.
datastores: Relational DB plus media storage.
auth: Admin auth and API tokens; external auth patterns possible.
apis: REST and GraphQL APIs, webhooks, admin plugins, and content workflows.
security: CMS admin is a privileged surface; media uploads and content API exposure need care.
setup_path: Only implement if the current site’s content model and editorial needs justify a dedicated CMS.
env: DB settings, JWT/app secrets, storage provider, admin URL, and webhook settings.
persistence: Back up DB and media storage; content migrations should be versioned carefully.
operations: Content model migrations, plugin updates, and admin security matter.
failure_points: Adding CMS complexity when content volume/editorial workflow does not justify it.
integration: Could front content for Next.js and possibly docs/help content, but is not automatically needed.
verify: Compare current content workflows against real CMS needs before adopting.
risk: Moderate duplication risk.
gdpr: Mostly editorial data unless user-generated content is added.
recommendation: ONLY IF CONTENT OPS OUTGROW CURRENT APPROACH
overlap: Overlaps with current Next.js content setup and partly with Docusaurus/BookStack for docs content.
codex_read_first: Read the current Hylono content architecture before recommending Strapi.
codex_best_path: Stick with current site architecture unless editorial requirements clearly demand a CMS.
codex_never_assume: Never introduce a CMS just because a site is content-heavy; validate real workflow pain first.

name: Jitsi
license: Apache-2.0 / mixed component licenses across ecosystem
what: Open-source video conferencing platform for meetings and embedded calls.
solves: Video meetings, calls, and lightweight conferencing with self-host control.
users: Teams and product builders needing meetings or embeddable calls without classroom complexity.
fit: Better fit than BigBlueButton for general Hylono video consultations, team meetings, or lightweight session calls.
runtime: Multi-service media stack with web app, XMPP/JVB components, TURN/STUN, and optional recording/streaming add-ons.
components: Web UI, prosody/Jicofo/JVB, media relays, auth options, and deployment scripts.
deploy: Official Docker deployment is the practical path. Still requires media-capacity and TURN/STUN planning.
deps: Compute, bandwidth, TURN/STUN, storage for recordings if enabled, TLS, and auth integration.
datastores: Config/state plus optional recording storage.
auth: Anonymous, token, or integrated auth modes depending on setup.
apis: Meet APIs, iframe/embed, JWT auth, recording integrations, and media services.
security: Meeting access, media traffic, recording storage, and public exposure need careful configuration.
setup_path: Use official docker-jitsi-meet releases and handbook. Good candidate if Hylono needs self-hosted meetings but not full classroom tooling.
env: Public URL, TURN/STUN, auth/JWT, media ports, recording settings, and TLS.
persistence: Back up config and recording storage if recordings are used.
operations: Capacity, NAT/TURN, upgrades, and meeting-access controls matter.
failure_points: Underestimating media ops or exposing meetings without proper auth.
integration: Good fit for consultations, internal meetings, or embedded session calls. Could integrate with Cal.com booking and site flows.
verify: Confirm whether self-hosted video is actually needed versus managed meeting tools.
risk: Moderate.
gdpr: Calls and recordings can be sensitive; consent and retention matter.
recommendation: PRIMARY SELF-HOST VIDEO CANDIDATE IF NEEDED
overlap: Preferred over BigBlueButton for general meetings.
codex_read_first: Read Jitsi and BBB together before recommending video infrastructure.
codex_best_path: Use Jitsi for simple meetings/consultations if self-hosting is really needed; otherwise avoid media ops burden.
codex_never_assume: Never assume self-hosted video is cheap to operate just because the software is open source.

name: Chatwoot
what: Open-source customer support platform for live chat, inboxes, agents, and messaging channels.
solves: Customer support inboxes, website chat, conversation assignment, and support workflows.
users: Support and success teams.
fit: Good fit if Hylono wants first-party support chat and unified inboxes. Stronger than building chat/support from scratch into the site.
runtime: Rails/Postgres/Redis-style application with websockets, workers, and channel integrations.
components: Web app, inboxes, agent UI, PostgreSQL, Redis, workers, and channel adapters.
deploy: Mature self-host docs exist. Production needs email/channel integrations, storage, and support operations.
deps: PostgreSQL, Redis, storage, mail/chat channel credentials, and background jobs.
datastores: PostgreSQL and Redis plus uploads storage.
auth: Agent/user auth and channel/customer identity within conversations.
apis: REST APIs, web widget, webhooks, and channel integrations.
security: Support conversations can contain PII and sensitive service details. Access control and retention matter.
setup_path: Good later-phase support tooling candidate. Start only when support volume justifies a dedicated platform.
env: DB/Redis settings, app secrets, storage, mail, channel creds, base URL, and worker config.
persistence: Back up DB and uploads; conversation history retention needs policy.
operations: Agent workflow, channel integrations, and response/SLA ownership matter.
failure_points: Adding support tooling before support process is defined, or duplicating CRM/support ownership badly.
integration: Can integrate with Next.js site chat widget, Twenty CRM, n8n, Novu, and support analytics.
verify: Decide whether support chat/inbox is strategic now or later.
risk: Moderate, but useful when customer support scales.
gdpr: Support transcripts are personal data; retention/access/export matter.
overlap: Can overlap with CRM notes and email inbox workflows, but fills a clear support role.
codex_read_first: Read current support-channel strategy before implementing.
codex_best_path: Use Chatwoot when support operations need a dedicated inbox/workflow platform.
codex_never_assume: Never deploy chat just because it looks modern; verify support staffing and SLAs first.

name: Mautic
what: Marketing automation platform for campaigns, segments, email journeys, forms, and lead nurturing.
solves: Advanced lifecycle marketing automation beyond simple newsletters or transactional notifications.
users: Marketing teams running complex nurture campaigns and multi-step journeys.
fit: Useful only if Hylono wants serious marketing automation. Otherwise Listmonk + Novu + n8n likely cover current needs with less overhead.
runtime: PHP application with DB, queues/cron, mail, and campaign/segment logic.
components: Campaign builder, contacts/segments, forms/emails, DB, cron/jobs, and integrations.
deploy: Self-host is possible but operationally heavier than Listmonk.
deps: Database, mail, cron/queue, storage, and marketing-process ownership.
datastores: Relational database.
auth: Admin users and API access.
apis: Marketing APIs, forms, webhooks, and campaign automations.
security: Marketing contacts, tracking, and consent handling require strong process governance.
setup_path: Only adopt if advanced nurture journeys become a real growth need.
env: DB settings, mail, app secrets, cron, storage, and tracking config.
persistence: Back up DB and templates/assets.
operations: Deliverability, campaign QA, consent management, and cron reliability matter.
failure_points: Overkill compared to simpler newsletter/transactional stack.
integration: Can integrate with site forms, CRM, n8n, and analytics if lifecycle marketing becomes strategic.
verify: Decide whether advanced marketing automation is really needed beyond Listmonk/Novu/n8n.
risk: Moderate overlap/complexity risk.
gdpr: Marketing consent and contact data handling are central.
recommendation: LATER / ONLY IF ADVANCED MARKETING AUTOMATION IS NEEDED
overlap: Heavily overlaps with Listmonk, Dittofeed, and some n8n/Novu workflows.
codex_read_first: Read the overlap map with Listmonk/Novu before planning Mautic.
codex_best_path: Use only when a real nurture-marketing program requires it.
codex_never_assume: Never adopt a marketing automation suite before the team/process is ready to operate it.

name: Formbricks
researched_as: Formbricks survey and experience management platform
what: Open-source survey, feedback, and experience management platform.
solves: Surveys, NPS, product feedback, intercepts, and research loops under self-host control.
users: Product, marketing, and research teams.
fit: Good fit for structured feedback and surveys. It complements PostHog rather than replacing it.
runtime: Web app with DB, analytics/response handling, and survey delivery surfaces.
components: Survey builder, response store, targeting, web/widget delivery, and admin UI.
deploy: Self-host docs are mature; HTTPS and modest resources are required.
deps: Database, storage, HTTPS, email if used, and event/targeting integrations.
datastores: Relational DB and response data.
auth: Admin/workspace users and survey delivery controls.
apis: Survey APIs, webhooks, and response/export surfaces.
security: Survey responses may contain personal or sensitive health-adjacent feedback. Scope surveys carefully.
setup_path: Good later addition for customer research, post-rental feedback, or NPS.
env: DB settings, app URL, HTTPS, secrets, mail, and survey targeting config.
persistence: Back up DB and response data; define retention for response history.
operations: Survey targeting, consent/context, and response analysis ownership matter.
failure_points: Using it to collect sensitive health-like data without proper framing and governance.
integration: Can integrate with Next.js site, PostHog, n8n, CRM, and email/notification systems.
verify: Define exactly what feedback loops Hylono needs and what data should never be asked.
risk: Low to moderate.
gdpr: Survey responses can become personal data or special-category adjacent depending on questions.
overlap: Complements PostHog and site forms; not a replacement for analytics.
codex_read_first: Read current feedback-collection methods and data-sensitivity rules first.
codex_best_path: Use Formbricks for bounded feedback/survey workflows once customer research becomes structured.
codex_never_assume: Never ask for health-sensitive data just because a survey tool makes it easy.

name: Gorse
what: Open-source recommendation engine for personalized recommendations and ranking.
solves: Recommendations such as related products/content/protocols based on behavior and metadata.
users: Product and data teams building recommendation features.
fit: Interesting later if Hylono wants personalization for content, products, or protocols. Not needed early.
runtime: Go services with cache/storage dependencies and recommendation APIs.
components: Master, worker, server, data store, cache, and recommendation models.
deploy: Self-host is feasible but adds data and relevance-tuning burden.
deps: Datastore, cache, event data, and recommendation-quality evaluation.
datastores: Databases/caches for feedback, items, and recommendation state.
auth: API/service auth patterns; verify current deployment model.
apis: Recommendation APIs and data import APIs.
security: Main risk is data quality and recommendation misuse rather than infrastructure exposure.
setup_path: Only add once Hylono has meaningful interaction data and a clear recommendation use case.
env: DB/cache settings, model/training config, service URLs, and API keys.
persistence: Back up metadata/state as needed; much value depends on event data pipelines.
operations: Data freshness, recommendation quality, and model tuning matter.
failure_points: Trying to personalize too early with weak data.
integration: Could later connect to product/content views and Next.js personalization APIs.
verify: Confirm whether recommendations are a real growth/product lever yet.

name: Temporal
what: Durable workflow orchestration platform for long-running, retryable, stateful business processes in code.
solves: Reliable orchestration of multi-step business processes that must survive retries, outages, and long waits.
users: Engineering teams building complex, durable workflows in code.
fit: Powerful but easy to over-adopt. Hylono should start with n8n and app-native workflows; Temporal becomes interesting only when business-critical long-running workflows outgrow those tools.
runtime: Server cluster with history/matching/frontend services plus database and optional search.
components: Temporal server services, persistence DB, task queues/workers in application code, and visibility/search components.
deploy: Self-host Docker/Kubernetes exists, but this is serious platform infrastructure, not a casual utility.
deps: PostgreSQL/MySQL/Cassandra depending on deployment, workers in code, monitoring, and durable queue thinking.
datastores: Persistence DB plus visibility/search components depending on setup.
auth: Service auth and application-level worker/client auth patterns.
apis: SDKs for workflow code, gRPC APIs, worker/task queues, and admin tooling.
security: Workflow payloads can include sensitive business data. Access, retention, and namespace boundaries matter.
setup_path: Do not adopt before exhausting simpler orchestration patterns.
env: Persistence DB, service config, namespace/auth, worker endpoints, and monitoring.
persistence: Back up persistence DB and treat workflow histories as durable state.
operations: Server upgrades, SDK compatibility, namespace management, and worker reliability matter.
failure_points: Using Temporal because it is powerful rather than because durable coded workflows are clearly needed.
integration: Could eventually power long-running rental/service/billing workflows in code, but not before n8n/app code hits limits.
verify: Identify actual workflows that require Temporal-grade durability and code orchestration.
risk: High complexity; high payoff only for the right problems.
gdpr: Workflow histories may store personal data; retention and minimization matter.
recommendation: LATE / ONLY FOR TRUE DURABLE-WORKFLOW NEEDS
overlap: Partial overlap with n8n and application job queues, but at a different sophistication level.
codex_read_first: Read n8n limitations and workflow-complexity notes before proposing Temporal.
codex_best_path: Defer until there is a clear set of long-running, failure-sensitive workflows that simpler tools cannot handle.
codex_never_assume: Never treat Temporal as a default queue or cron replacement.

solves: Connecting to U.S. healthcare records and normalizing patient data from HIEs/providers.
users: Healthcare software teams needing interoperability with U.S. healthcare systems.
fit: Poor fit for Hylono’s current EU wellness stack. Strategic mismatch unless the company enters regulated healthcare interoperability, especially in the U.S.
runtime: Healthcare integration platform with API services, data normalization, and provider/network connectors.
components: API layer, healthcare connectors, data normalization pipelines, and auth/integration management.
deploy: Self-hosting is possible but domain-specific and compliance-heavy.
deps: Healthcare connectivity, auth, secure storage, and domain expertise.
datastores: Healthcare data stores and integration metadata.
auth: API auth and healthcare-integration credential handling.
apis: FHIR/C-CDA/PDF-related APIs and provider connectivity endpoints.
security: Potential PHI and regulated-health data. Very high governance burden.

name: Wazuh
what: Open-source security platform / SIEM/XDR-style stack for log analysis, endpoint monitoring, integrity checks, and compliance use cases.
solves: Security monitoring, alerting, endpoint telemetry, and central security visibility.
users: Security and infrastructure teams operating many hosts/services.
fit: Useful later if Hylono’s self-hosted footprint grows enough to justify centralized security operations. Not a first-wave requirement.
runtime: Manager/indexer/dashboard/agent architecture with significant operational footprint.
components: Agents, manager, indexer, dashboard, rules, and security analytics pipelines.
deploy: Self-hostable but operationally heavy. Best introduced when there are enough systems to secure centrally.
deps: Compute/storage, agent management, network design, rules tuning, and security operations ownership.
datastores: Indexer/search cluster and associated metadata.
auth: Dashboard/admin auth and agent trust relationships.
apis: Security APIs, agent communication, alerting, and dashboards.
security: Security tooling itself becomes critical infrastructure; access and update hygiene matter.
setup_path: Defer until the platform footprint and security maturity justify centralized SIEM/EDR-style tooling.
env: Cluster/indexer settings, certificates, agent enrollment, dashboard auth, and storage sizing.
persistence: Security logs and indexes require retention policy and storage planning.
operations: Rule tuning, false positives, agent lifecycle, and incident workflows dominate.
failure_points: Deploying SIEM tooling before there is a team/process to operate it.
integration: Could later cover hosts running databases, gateways, app clusters, and critical services.
verify: Decide when Hylono’s infra/security maturity is ready for central security operations.
risk: High ops burden if premature.
gdpr: Security logs can contain personal/system identifiers; retention/access matter.
overlap: Some overlap with cloud-native security monitoring, but broader self-host visibility if adopted.
codex_read_first: Read the security-operations maturity notes before recommending Wazuh.
codex_best_path: Defer until there is enough infrastructure and ownership to justify it.
codex_never_assume: Never deploy SIEM/EDR-style platforms without people/process to run them.
